summary:
The DETECT Fentanyl and Xylazine Act of 2024 (Public Law 118-186) directs the Science and Technology Directorate of the Department of Homeland Security to develop improved capabilities for detecting and identifying illicit drugs, including those present in very low concentrations, such as fentanyl and xylazine.

The bill amends Section 302 of the Homeland Security Act of 2002 by adding a new responsibility. In coordination with the Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA), the Directorate must conduct research, development, testing, evaluation, and cost-benefit analyses to improve the safety, effectiveness, and efficiency of detection equipment and the reference libraries used by federal, state, local, Tribal, and territorial law enforcement agencies. This work specifically includes developing: (1) portable equipment capable of detecting and identifying drugs with minimal or no handling of the sample; (2) equipment that can separate complex mixtures containing low drug concentrations and high concentrations of cutting agents to enable field identification; and (3) technologies using machine learning or artificial intelligence (as defined in the National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act of 2020) to predict whether substances in a sample are controlled substance analogues or new psychoactive substances not yet listed in existing reference libraries.

In carrying out these activities, the Under Secretary for Science and Technology must follow the recommendations and best practices in the National Institute of Standards and Technology's Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework (NIST AI 100-1) or any successor document. The Under Secretary must also set the Directorate's research and evaluation priorities based on the latest available information, including drugs identified as threats in the most recent Homeland Threat Assessment, the DEA's State and Territory Report on Enduring and Emerging Threats, or successor documents.

The Act includes a rule of construction stating that nothing in it limits the authority of agencies already managing or involved in drug equipment and reference libraries. The law was approved on December 23, 2024.
